original_hypothesis.title: Systematic Contestation Griefing to Delay L2 Finality
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: An attacker can systematically contest every valid proof at the lowest tier, forcing resolution at higher tiers. Each contest delays verification by requiring a higher-tier proof + cooldown window. On mainnet: SGX contest (500 TKO) → needs SGX_ZKVM resolution (24h cooldown). SGX_ZKVM contest (1000 TKO) → needs Guardian resolution (1h cooldown). Total cost per block: 1500 TKO. Total delay per block: ~25+ hours.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The contestation mechanism allows any party to delay block verification for ~25 hours per block for 1500 TKO. At scale, this can prevent L2 finality indefinitely, blocking bridge withdrawals and cross-chain operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contest path only requires posting contestBond and different blockHash/stateRoot; no proof verification needed at same tier

preconditions: Attacker has sufficient TKO to post contest bonds (500-1000 TKO per block)
Verifier skips proof verification when isContesting is true (SgxVerifier returns early)
Higher-tier provers exist to eventually resolve

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: SGX contestBond=500 TKO, SGX_ZKVM contestBond=1000 TKO; contester loses full bond when proven wrong (minus 25% to winner)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Contest every block at SGX tier (500 TKO each); if needed, contest SGX_ZKVM resolution (1000 TKO); each adds 24h+ delay
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly profitable; griefing cost is 1500 TKO/block. For 864,000 blocks (max buffer), cost would be 1.296B TKO.

economic_questions: What is the cost per hour of finality delay?
Is the contestation bond sufficient deterrent at current TKO prices?
Can automated systems escalate proofs fast enough to minimize delay?
validation_requirements: Calculate total delay from double-contestation (SGX + SGX_ZKVM)
Verify SgxVerifier.verifyProof returns early on contesting without proof check
Model optimal attacker strategy for maximum delay per TKO spent

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Delayed L2 finality reduces confidence and bridge usability

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All L2 users awaiting finality for bridge withdrawals
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending bridge operations during delay period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: SGX cooldown=1440 min, SGX_ZKVM cooldown=1440 min, Guardian cooldown=60 min
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model full contest → resolution → verification timeline

validation_result.impact_summary: The described behavior is the intended design of the optimistic proving system, where economic bonds deter griefing.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that an attacker can systematically contest valid proofs to delay L2 finality by paying the contest bonds, which forces resolution at higher tiers and triggers new cooldown windows. While the mechanics described in the hypothesis are accurate, this is not a code defect. It is the explicit, intended design of the Taiko protocol's tiered proving system.

The protocol is an optimistic rollup design where any party can challenge a state transition by posting a `contestBond`. To facilitate permissionless contesting, the code explicitly skips proof verification when `isContesting` is true (as seen in `SgxVerifier.sol` and commented `// Do not run proof verification to contest an existing proof`). When a contest occurs, `LibVerifying.sol` correctly halts verification for that block. When a higher-tier proof resolves the contest, it correctly resets the `timestamp`, enforcing the higher tier's cooldown window.

The economic cost of 1500 TKO per block (500 for SGX, 1000 for SGX_ZKVM) is the designed deterrent against this exact griefing vector. If an attacker executes this, they will lose their entire bond (75% burned, 25% to the honest prover). There are no missing checks, incorrect formulas, or logic errors; the smart contracts correctly enforce the rules and penalties of the economic game.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code explicitly implements the contestation mechanism as designed. `SgxVerifier.sol` contains an explicit check `if (_ctx.isContesting) return;` with a comment confirming this is intended. `LibProving.sol` correctly transfers the `contestBond` from the attacker and sets `ts.contester`. `LibVerifying.sol` correctly halts verification when `ts.contester != address(0)`. The system is functioning exactly as intended.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: Not applicable. The execution path follows the intended design for handling block contestations.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is technically possible but represents the intended functioning of the protocol's economic security model. The attacker must pay the full `contestBond` for every block they wish to delay, and they will lose this bond when honest provers submit higher-tier proofs. This is the standard mechanism of optimistic rollups, not a code defect.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No vulnerability exists. The protocol relies on the economic cost (1500 TKO per block) to make this griefing attack prohibitively expensive at scale.
recommended_mitigations: No code changes required. The protocol's security relies on ensuring the `contestBond` values in the tier providers are economically significant enough to deter griefing.
